name: Stalin Scheme Compiler
summary: Jeffrey Mark Siskind's Stalin (Scheme to native via C, 1991+): whole-program type inference, control-flow analysis, escape analysis, and constant propagation run over the entire program before code generation. C code competitive with hand-written C on numeric benchmarks. Super-linear compile times; no separate compilation; no eval.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Jeffrey Mark Siskind's **Stalin** (Scheme to native via C, 1991+) takes whole-program optimisation to a research extreme: **type inference, control-flow analysis, escape analysis, and constant propagation are all run over the entire program** — including all libraries — before code generation. The compiler can elide allocation entirely for objects proven not to escape, specialise generic operations to monomorphic call sites, unbox numbers throughout, and emit C code competitive with hand-written C on numeric benchmarks.

Siskind reports 0.6–4× of `gcc -O2` C performance on the SPEC suite, achieved purely through aggressive whole-program inference rather than runtime specialisation.

sections.title: Architectural cost
sections.content:
The architectural cost is uncompromising:

- **No separate compilation**: Stalin requires the whole program to be available at compile time. There is no library boundary; the compiler must see every definition.
- **No dynamic loading or eval**: extending Scheme's reflective semantics is impossible because the type-inference algorithm relies on closed-world reasoning.
- **Super-linear compilation time**: type inference scales poorly with program size. Stalin times out on some programs and falls back to less aggressive specialisation; large benchmarks can take minutes to hours to compile.
- **Undecidable in general**: the underlying type-inference algorithm (a flow-sensitive form of Henglein's quartic-time inference adapted to Scheme) is undecidable in the worst case.

sections.title: Lesson — upper bound on whole-program inference
sections.content:
The relevance to a new language is the **upper bound on what whole-program compilation can achieve** for a dynamically-typed source language. Stalin shows that Scheme's lack of language-level type information can be substantially recovered by whole-program inference, but only at compilation costs unacceptable for interactive development.

Virgil takes a similar whole-program-reachability-first approach with weaker optimisation and faster compilation; Stalin shows the opposite trade-off in the same design space; SBCL is the production middle ground that gives up some specialisation power in exchange for incremental compilation. Status (as of 2026-04): research-grade. Stalin is no longer actively developed but the techniques continue to influence whole-program optimisers.
